## How to Make Rasmalai (Stepwise Photos)
### Make Rabri
1. **Making rabri**. Pour 3 cups milk to a heavy bottom pot. Bring it to a boil on a medium flame. Add saffron/ kesar and keep stirring every few minutes to prevent burning.
![making rabri with milk for rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
2. Next pour sugar. I used organic sugar. You can use white sugar as well.
![adding sugar for rabri in rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
3. Adjust the flame as desired to suit your convenience. Do not use high flame milk may overflow the pot. Keep stirring every 2 to 3 minutes, moving aside the malai/cream that comes on top. Turn off when it gets condensed to half the quantity. 
Chill half of this in the fridge. Keep the rest in the same pot.
![saffron flavored condensed milk for rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
### Make Chenna 
4. While the rabri is getting done, bring 5 cups milk to a boil in another pot for making chenna/paneer. If you are using non-homogenized milk then take off the milk from the stove and allow to rest for 5 mins. Then add the vinegar.
If using homogenized milk, add the curd or lemon juice or vinegar once the milk comes to a boil. 
![boiling milk for chenna in rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
5. Keep stirring until the milk curdles completely. If needed add more lemon juice or vinegar. Switch off the stove.
![curdling milk](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
6. When you see it has curdled completely, immediately add ice cubes or ice cold water. This is very important to keep the chenna soft.
![addition of ice cubes](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
7. Drain it up in a thin clean muslin cloth.
![draining chenna in cloth](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
8. Wrap up the paneer in the cloth bringing together all the edges. Put the cloth under running water, making sure the paneer is wrapped well in the cloth. Rinse it well under the running water to remove the smell of lemon or vinegar. 
Tie up the paneer in the cloth like shown in the picture. Squeeze off the excess water. Hang it up on a hook for 45 minutes to 1 hour for the whey to completely drain.
![hanging paneer for draining in rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
9. At this step you must have chenna that is just moist with no dripping water and not sticky. Knead it well until you get smooth, grain free and non-sticky chena. You must not knead to the extent that chenna begins to release grease or fats.
Please check the pictures I have shared in the intro to know the right texture.
![kneading paneer for rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
### Make Rasmalai
10. Divide it to 12 equal portions. Make balls and flatten them gently. You can also make rasgulla or chum chum. If you find the edges cracked up, gently smoothen them. Cover and set them aside.
![making balls discs for rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
11. Bring 3 1/2 cups water to a boil with 1 cup sugar. Use refined white sugar.
![boiling sugar syrup](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
12. When the syrup begins to boil, add cardamom powder.
![addition of elaichi](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
13. Next when the syrup begins to boil rapidly, add flattened discs of chenna. Cover immediately and cook on a moderately high flame for exactly 9 minutes.
![boiling discs in sugar syrup for rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
14. The discs would have doubled in size. Keep it covered and take off the pot from the stove. Rest for atleast 20 mins.
![cooked balls in syrup for rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
15. Take out one by one and cool. Gently squeeze them in between your palms to remove the absorbed sugar syrup. You can also do it with a spatula and spoon.
![sugar syrup](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
16. Add them to the warm rabri.
![rabri](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
17. Add chopped pistachios, almonds and cashews. You can also simmer for just one to 2 minutes on a low flame for the milk to be absorbed by the discs. Do not overcook at this stage, they may break. 
Pour the chilled rabri to this and serve rasmalai chilled.
![Add chopped pistachios](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201200%201200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
## Method 2 – Easy Rasmalai in 10 minutes
Using ready made rasgulla and milkmaid.
**Ingredients**
  * 12 rasgullas
  * 1 tin milk maid condensed milk (392 grams)
  * 2 cups milk
  * ⅛ Tsp. Green cardamom powder / elaichi
  * Few saffron strands
  * Chopped Pistachios and almonds for garnish


### Instructions
1. You can use any ready made rasgulla. Squeeze the syrup out of the balls. Do not press them too hard, they will break. Make sure that the syrup is extracted from the balls else they will not absorb the milk. Set these aside.
![easy rasmalai recipe using condensed milk](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
2. Pour milk and condensed milk in a pan and bring it to a boil.
![making easy rasmalai recipe with condensed milk](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
3. Add saffron and cardamom powder. Simmer it for around 5 to 6 mins so that the milk blends well with milkmaid. Alternately, You can melt the saffron in little hot milk and add too.
![Add saffron and cardamom powder](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
4. Reduce the flame to medium. Add the rasgulla to the condensed milk.
![Reduce the flame to medium](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
5. Cover with a lid and simmer for a min or two. Overcooking can break them.
6. Easy rasmalai using rasgulla is ready in less than 10 minutes.  
Garnish with nuts. You could add the chopped nuts at step 4 too.
Note: It will be sufficient even if you use ¾ th can of condensed milk (392 gms) and 1.5 cups of milk. We have a sweet tooth, so I use up the entire can and we enjoy the creamy milk too. So adjust to suit your need.
![rasgulla for easy rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20670%20447'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![rasmalai recipe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20680%20510'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
## How to make super soft chena?
**Boiling & curdling milk correctly** will give to the super soft chena. If the chena is not soft then rasmalai will not turn soft but instead will turn hard.
The first step is to bring the milk to a boil & then curdle it to make soft chena. **Well there are 2 methods to curdle milk to make soft chena**. 
**Method 1** – **Using homogenized &/or pasteurized milk**: This first method is to curdle the milk immediately after the milk comes to a boil whilst milk is still on the stove. 
Curdle first by adding the acidic ingredient & then turn off the stove. Immediately pour ice cold water or add ice cubes to stop it from cooking further. 
This ice cold water or ice cubes will keep your chena super soft as it stops further cooking. This method is best if you are using pasteurized or/& homogenized milk. 
This method has consistently yielded me soft chena. Do not follow this method if you are using non-homogenized milk or buffalo milk.
**If you follow this method using non-homogenized milk then you will end up with rubbery & hard chena.**
**Method 2** – **Using non-homogenized milk (not packet milk, but fresh from milkman)** : This method is to bring the milk to a boil. Turn off the stove and rest for about 5 to 10 minutes to cool down the milk slightly. Then curdle the milk by adding the acidic ingredient. 
This method will work if your milk is unprocessed & is pure without any additives. 
**This method will not work if you are using pasteurized & homogenized milk. Infact the milk will not curdle at all unless you continue to boil the milk.**
But back in India this is the method most people follow as they use non-homogenized milk. I have seen my mum following this method to make the softest paneer.
## Tips
Now my tip is to bring the milk to a boil, then turn off the stove and try adding the acidic ingredient. If the milk doesn’t curdle then put it back on the stove and continue to boil until the milk curdles. 
But remember to use ice cold water or ice cubes to stop the cooking further after the entire milk has curdled. This will give you the soft chena.
## Faqs
1. **Why does my rasmalai turn hard?**
Hard chena, over kneading the chena, boiling the chena discs for too long will result in hard & dense rasmalai.
2. **Why do my rasmalai or chena discs break or dissolve in sugar syrup?**
Too much whey in the chena will dissolve them in the syrup while boiling.
3. **How long do I need to hang the chena to drain whey?**
This depends of the kind of milk used. If using non-homogenized milk 45 to 60 mins is just good enough. If using homogenized then it depends again on the kind of milk. 
Some may take up to 6 hours. So do not go by the time go by the texture which I have explained below.
4. **What is the right texture of chena after draining?**
When the chena is still moist yet has no dripping whey in it then it is good for kneading. Here is a picture with details. 
I consider chena to be perfect if it passes this test. Take a small portion of the chena and smear it to a smooth surface like your kitchen countertop or chopping board.
Chena must be non-sticky & grainy or crumbly. Very sticky chena indicates too much moisture . So hang it for longer. 
Too grainy, dry or very crumbly chena indicates over drained. In this case you can knead with a moist hand. You can also dip your hand in whey as many times as required and knead.
![chena for rasmalai](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20375'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
5. **How long to knead chena for soft rasmalai?**
The main purpose of kneading chena is to make it smooth and soft without being grainy. So do not go by the clock, knead it until you get a soft, crumb free and smooth texture.
Over all the time it takes to knead depends on how grainy your paneer is. More acidic ingredient can make your chena more grainy. So just knead until your chena is crumb free and soft.
![kneading chena for rasmalai](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20375'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

## Rasmalai Recipe
4.99 from 314 votes
Rasmalai is a popular Bengali sweet delicacy made with Indian cottage cheese or chenna (paneer) discs. These discs are cooked in sugar syrup first to make them fluffy. Later served with chilled thickened milk. Rasmalai is garnished with chopped nuts. This recipe will give you the best soft and mouth melting rasmalai.

### Ingredients (US cup = 240ml )
#### To make Chenna
  * ▢ 1¼ liter milk or 5 cups full fat milk for chenna
  * ▢ 1 to 2 tbsp lemon juice  or vinegar (mix with 2 to 3 tbsp water) or 4 to 6 tbsp curd
  * ▢ 1 liter ice water  or ice cubes


#### For Sugar Syrup
  * ▢ 1 cup refined white sugar
  * ▢ 3½ cups water
  * ▢ ¼ tsp cardamom powder  or elaichi powder


#### To make Rabri
  * ▢ ¾ liter milk (3 cups full fat)
  * ▢ ½ cup sugar for rabri
  * ▢ pinch saffron or kesar (optional)
  * ▢ 2 tbsp pista  or almonds sliced


### Instructions
#### Make Rabri (Thickened Milk)
  * ▢ 
Pour 3 cups milk to a heavy bottom pot and boil. 
  * ▢ 
Add saffron and sugar when the milk comes to a boil. 
  * ▢ 
Reduce the flame to medium. Stir every 2 to 3 minutes to prevent the milk from scorching at the bottom. 
  * ▢ 
When a layer of cream forms, just move it aside. 
  * ▢ 
Boil until the milk thickens and reduces to half the quantity. Set this aside to cool. 
  * ▢ 
Cool half of this and chill in the fridge. Keep the other half warm.


#### Make chenna
  * ▢ 
Bring 5 cups milk to a boil in a pot. Turn off the stove.
  * ▢ 
Pour lemon juice and keep stirring until the milk has curdled completely.
  * ▢ 
If it doesn't curdle then add some more and stir. 
  * ▢ 
Next if it fails to curdle then turn on the stove & regulate to a low flame. Stir until it curdles.
  * ▢ 
Turn off the stove. Pour ice water or add ice cubes to the pot and set aside for 2 mins. 
  * ▢ 
Drain to a muslin cloth lined over a colander.
  * ▢ 
Pour fresh water over the chena and rinse well. You can also do it under running water for the sour taste to vanish. 
  * ▢ 
Wrap the chena in the cloth. Squeeze off excess whey and make a knot.
  * ▢ 
Next hang it for about 45 minutes to 1 hour for the excess whey to drain off.
  * ▢ 
Chena should not have any excess dripping whey & should not be completely dry. It must be moist and grainy but not very sticky or dry. Please check the images above the recipe card.


#### Kneading chena
  * ▢ 
Transfer the chenna to a plate. At this stage chenna should not have any excess whey in it. 
  * ▢ 
Knead well for about 3 to 5 minutes until smooth. Chenna should not be grainy but must be smooth. Do not go by the time but focus on the texture – smooth, non grainy and non-sticky texture is required.
  * ▢ 
Make 12 equal balls and flatten them to make discs. 
  * ▢ 
If the discs are slightly cracked on the sides, roll the sides gently. Set these aside.


#### Sugar syrup
  * ▢ 
Bring 3½ cups of water to a boil with 1 cup sugar. 
  * ▢ 
Stir until the sugar dissolves completely. Then add cardamom powder.
  * ▢ 
When the sugar syrup begins to boil rapidly, then add the discs gently. 
  * ▢ 
Cook covered for about 9 to 10 minutes on a moderately high flame.
  * ▢ 
Turn off the stove. Remove the pot from the stove to avoid cooking further.
  * ▢ 
Keep the lid closed for the next 20 mins to avoid them turning flat or shrinking.
  * ▢ 
When done they will sink in the syrup. 


#### How to Make Rasmalai 
  * ▢ 
Take off the discs to a plate and cool them. 
  * ▢ 
Squeeze them gently in between your palms to remove sugar syrup.
  * ▢ 
Add these to the half of the warm thickened milk or rabri. 
  * ▢ 
Rest them for 3 to 4 hours. Pour the other half of the chilled rabri to this just before serving.
  * ▢ 
Garnish rasmalai with chopped nuts.

Cristina Ilicea 
Hi,  
I’ve tried twice already and my chenna discs keep dissolving.  
First attempt was with store bought fresh cheese and I thought making my chenna from scratch would work.  
But even now making chenna from scratch resulted in very sticky cheese even after kneading for more than 8min.  
Are there any suggestions related to the milk to be used when making chenna?  
I used full fat non homogenised milk, pasteurised at mild temperature, not UHT.  
Would it work better with raw milk?
Reply
swasthi 
[ Cristina Ilicea ](https://www.indianhealthyrecipes.com/rasmalai-recipe-easy-rasmalai-in-10-mins-diwali-recipes/#comment-185063)
Hi Cristina,  
It is not advised to use store bought cheese to make rasmalai. Raw milk is the best choice and that’s what is used in India. But where I live raw milk is not available by law and I’ve been making rasmalai for many years using pasturized milk. It works well. I know they disintegrate if there is excess moisture in the chenna. If the chena is sticky it means there is too much moisture and if it is slimy it means the milk was not boiled and curdled well. If the problem was not slime and is just sticky from excess moisture, it should have been drained well. I’ve also found adding the acidic ingredient too early without boiling the milk can retain a lot of moisture in the chenna. To troubleshoot  
1.either use raw milk or switch to another brand of pasturized milk  
2. Heat the milk really well to the extent where it may overlong if you heat any longer.  
3. Drain the cheese longer.  
4. Knead only until smooth but don’t overkneed  
5. Make sure you add them to the boiling syrup else they will disintegrate  
6. You may try adding a tsp of corn starch but that will change the texture and it may turn chewy.  
Hope this helps
Reply

Shila 
I cooked my ras also in syrup for 9 mins and turned off the heat and covered it for 20 min but the syrup is not soaked in . The rasmalai is hard. Is there a solution to save it?
Reply
swasthi 
[ Shila ](https://www.indianhealthyrecipes.com/rasmalai-recipe-easy-rasmalai-in-10-mins-diwali-recipes/#comment-156983)
Hi Shila,  
If the syrup is not boiling hot the discs won’t puff. It must be bubbling before adding them and continue to boil the same way even after adding the discs. If the temperature drops they won’t turn light and will not soak up the syrup. Instead they will be hard. As soon as you add the discs, the temperature comes down but if the syrup is not boiling any longer, increase the temperature while you add the discs and reduce back to keep it boiling when you cover the pot. Give a few more tries. It takes a few trials to learn making rasmalai correctly. Hope this helps.
Reply

## Latest Ranchi News in Hindi :
रांची के स्थानीय समाचार, राजनीतिक, एजुकेशन, स्वास्थ्य, लाइफ स्टाइल, मौसम, इवेंट, रेलवे, बिजनेस और प्रशासनिक खबरों की हर पल की ताजा जानकारी पाने के लिए आप प्रभात खबर से जरूर जुड़े रहें। यहां आपको निष्पक्ष समाचार, लाइव रिपोर्टिंग, फैक्ट चेक, ग्राउंड रिपोर्ट के साथ खबरों की विश्वनीयता हर पल सुनिश्चित की जाती है। यहां पर हर समय आपको प्रभात खबर के 100 से अधिक रिपोर्ट्स, संवाद सूत्र, एक्सपर्ट्स और विषय विशेषज्ञ की खोजपरक रिपोर्टिंग, लेख, फीचर, टिप्पणी भी रियल टाइम में और मुक्त मिलती है।
### About Ranchi
रांची (Ranchi), झारखंड की राजधानी है और यह अपनी खूबसूरत “City of Waterfalls” के नाम से भी मशहूर है। यह शहर सिर्फ झारखंड की राजधानी की वजह से नहीं, बल्कि प्राकृतिक खूबसूरती, हरी भरी वादियों और पारंपरिक आदिवासी संस्कृतियों के लिए भी सदियों से जाना जाता आया है। रांची महान स्वतंत्रता सेनानी बिरसा मुंडा जी की धरती होने के कारण ऐतिहासिक महत्व भी रखती है। यहां की पावन धरती पर संस्कृति, शिक्षा, उद्योग और पर्यटन सबका अनोखा मेल देखने को मिलता है।

### रांची का मौसम और खानपान (Ranchi Weather & Food)
बात जब रांची शहर की मौसम की आए तो यहां का मौसम पूरे साल सुहाना रहता है। यहां गर्मियों में तापमान 25°C से लेकर 38°C तक जाता है। वही बात सर्दियों की जाए तो यह 10°C तक गिर जाता है और खासकर जब बात मानसून के समय की जाए तो यहां के झरनों की खूबसूरती का नजारा देखने लायक होती है जो सबको अपनी तरफ आकर्षित कर लेती है।
रही बात रांची की खानपान की तो यहां के खाने का नाम सुनते ही लोगों के मुंह में पानी आ जाता है। यहां का दही चूड़ा, लिट्टी चोखा, पीठा, हांडी मटन, सत्तू पराठा और धूसका देश के साथ विदेश तक बेहद लोकप्रिय है और रही बात यहां की मिठाइयों की तो ठेकुआ और गुजिया के स्वाद को कोई कैसे भूल जाता है, इनके स्वाद का हर कोई दीवाना है।

**Jharkhand** ([Hindi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language"): ___Jhārkhaṇḍ___ , pronounced [[d͡ʒʱaːɾkʰəɳɖ]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu "Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu") [](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/8/86/LL-Q1568_%28hin%29-Flashthomsom-Jharkhand.wav/LL-Q1568_%28hin%29-Flashthomsom-Jharkhand.wav.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:LL-Q1568_\(hin\)-Flashthomsom-Jharkhand.wav "File:LL-Q1568 \(hin\)-Flashthomsom-Jharkhand.wav"); lit.'the land of forests') is a landlocked [state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") in [eastern India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_India "East India").[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-15) The state shares borders with the states of [West Bengal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") to the east, [Chhattisgarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhattisgarh "Chhattisgarh") to the west, [Uttar Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") to the northwest, [Bihar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") to the north and [Odisha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Odisha "Odisha") to the south. It is the [15th largest state by area](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_India_by_area "List of states and territories of India by area"), and the [14th largest by population](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_states_and_union_territories_of_India_by_population "List of states and union territories of India by population"). [Hindi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") is the official language of the state.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-NCLM52nd-5) The city of [Ranchi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi "Ranchi") is its capital, and [Dumka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dumka "Dumka") its sub-capital. The state is known for its waterfalls, hills and holy places; [Baidyanath Dham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baidyanath_Temple "Baidyanath Temple"), [Parasnath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parasnath "Parasnath"), [Dewri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maa_Dewri_Temple "Maa Dewri Temple") and [Rajrappa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajrappa "Rajrappa") are major religious sites.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-nyoooz.com-16)[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-17) As per 2011 census, Jharkhand is primarily rural, with about 24% of its population living in cities.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-18)
Jharkhand suffers from what is sometimes termed a [resource curse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resource_curse "Resource curse"): it accounts for more than 40% of [India's mineral production](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mining_in_India "Mining in India") but 39.1% of its population is below the poverty line and 19.6% of children under five years of age are malnourished.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-20)[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-21)
## Etymology
The word "_Jhar_ " means 'forest' and "_Khand_ " means 'land' in various [Indo-Aryan languages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"). Thus, "Jharkhand" means 'the land of forests'.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-22)
In the [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") epic [Mahabharata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata"), the region was referred as _Kark Khand_ due to its location near _Kark Rekha_ , that is, [Tropic of Cancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer").[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-23) It was also known as _Ark Khand_.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-24) During the Medieval period, the region was known as _Jharkhand_. According to [Bhavishya Purana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhavishya_Purana "Bhavishya Purana") (1200 CE), Jharkhand was one of the seven [Pundra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pundra "Pundra") desa. The name is first found on a 13th-century copper plate in [Kendrapada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kendrapara_district "Kendrapara district"), Odisha, from the reign of Narasimha Deva II of [Eastern Ganga dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eastern_Ganga_dynasty "Eastern Ganga dynasty"). Forest land from [Baidhnath dham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baidyanath_Temple "Baidyanath Temple") to [Puri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puri "Puri") was known as Jharkhand. In [Akbarnama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akbarnama "Akbarnama"), from [Panchet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panchet "Panchet") in the east to [Ratanpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ratanpur "Ratanpur") to west, [Rohtasgarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rohtasgarh "Rohtasgarh") to the north and the frontier of Odisha to the south was known as Jharkhand.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Gautam_Kumar_Bera-2008-25)[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-26)
## History
Main article: [History of Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Jharkhand "History of Jharkhand")
### Ancient period
The region has been inhabited since the Mesolithic-Chalcolithic period, as shown by several ancient cave paintings.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Publications_Division,_Ministry_of_Information_&_Broadcasting-2016-27)[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Cave_paintings_lie_in_neglect-28)[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Pearson_Education_India-29) Stone tools have been discovered from Chota Nagpur Plateau region which are from the [Mesolithic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") and [Neolithic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") periods.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Publications_Division,_Ministry_of_Information_&_Broadcasting-2016-27) There are ancient cave paintings in Isko, [Hazaribagh district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hazaribagh_district "Hazaribagh district") which are from Meso-chalcolithic period (9000–5000 BCE).[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Cave_paintings_lie_in_neglect-28) During 2nd millennium BCE the use of Copper tools spread in [Chota Nagpur Plateau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Plateau "Chota Nagpur Plateau") and these find complexes are known as the [Copper Hoard Culture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copper_Hoard_Culture "Copper Hoard Culture").[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-30) In the Kabra-Kala mound at the confluence of the [Son](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Son_River "Son River") and [North Koel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Koel_River "North Koel River") rivers in [Palamu district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palamu_district "Palamu district") various antiquities and art objects have been found which are from the [Neolithic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") to the medieval period and [pot-sherds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sherd "Sherd") of [Redware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Redware "Redware"), [black and red ware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_and_red_ware_culture "Black and red ware culture"), black ware, [black slipped ware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slipware "Slipware") and [NBP ware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Northern_Black_Polished_Ware "Northern Black Polished Ware") are from [Chalcolithic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") to the late medieval period.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-31) Several [iron slags](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slag "Slag"), [microliths](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microlith "Microlith"), and [potsherds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potsherds "Potsherds") have been discovered from [Singhbhum district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singhbhum_district "Singhbhum district") that are from 1400 BCE according to carbon dating age.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Pearson_Education_India-29) The region was ruled by many empires and dynasties including [Nanda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanda_Empire "Nanda Empire"), [Maurya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire") and [Gupta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire") during ancient period. 
In the Mahabharata, the region was referred as Kark Khand due to its location near Tropic of Cancer.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Nagpuri_Shist_Sahitya-32) During the age of Mahajanpadas around 500 BCE, Jharkhand state was a part of [Magadha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magadha_\(Mahajanapada\) "Magadha \(Mahajanapada\)") and [Anga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anga "Anga").[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-33)[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] In the Mauryan period, this region was ruled by a number of states, which were collectively known as the Atavika (forest) states. These states were subdued and were forced to accept the [hegemony](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") of the [Maurya Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire") during [Ashoka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka")'s reign (c. 232 BCE). In the ancient site of [Saridkel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saridkel "Saridkel"), burnt brick houses, red ware pottery, copper tools, coins and iron tools have been found which belong to the early centuries CE.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-34) Brahmi inscriptions have been found in Khunti district which are from the 3rd century BCE.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-35) [Samudragupta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samudragupta "Samudragupta"), while marching through the present-day Chotanagpur region ([North](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Chotanagpur_division "North Chotanagpur division") and [South](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Chotanagpur_division "South Chotanagpur division")), directed the first attack against the kingdom of [Dakshina Kosala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dakshina_Kosala "Dakshina Kosala") in the [Mahanadi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahanadi "Mahanadi") valley.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-36)
### Medieval period
In the 7th century, the Chinese traveller [Xuanzang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang") passed through the region. He described the kingdom as [Karnasuvarna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karnasuvarna "Karnasuvarna"), with [Shashanka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shashanka "Shashanka") as its ruler. To the north of Karn-Suberna was Magadha, Champa was in the east, Mahendra in the west, and Orissa in the south.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-37)
During the medieval period, the region was governed by [Nagvanshi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nagvanshi_dynasty "Nagvanshi dynasty"), [Pala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire"), [Khayaravala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khayaravala_dynasty "Khayaravala dynasty"), [Ramgarh Raj](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramgarh_Raj "Ramgarh Raj") and [Chero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chero_dynasty "Chero dynasty") rulers.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoma_Niyogi1959119-38)[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-https-39) A Buddhist monastery has been found in [Hazaribagh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hazaribagh_district "Hazaribagh district") which was built during the Pala period in the 10th century.[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-40) [Bhim Karn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhim_Karn "Bhim Karn") was a Nagvanshi king during medieval period. He defeated the [Raksel dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raksel_dynasty "Raksel dynasty") of Surguja when they invaded the region with cavalry.[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-41)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Khakparta_Temple.jpg/250px-Khakparta_Temple.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Khakparta_Temple.jpg)Khakparta Temple, a 9th-century Shiva temple in Lohardaga
### Modern period
[Mughal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") influence reached [Palamu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palamu_district "Palamu district") during the reign of Emperor [Akbar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akbar "Akbar") when it was conquered by [Rajput](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajput "Rajput") [Raja Mansingh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raja_Mansingh "Raja Mansingh") in 1574. Several invasions took place during Mughal rule.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-latehar.nic.in-42) During the reign of the Nagvanshi King [Madhu Singh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madhu_Singh_\(king\) "Madhu Singh \(king\)"), Akbar's general invaded [Khukhra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khukhragarh "Khukhragarh"). Also there was an invasion during the reign of [Durjan Shah](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Durjan_Shah "Durjan Shah").[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-43)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Map_of_present-day_Jharkhand_region_by_Rennell%2C_1779.jpg/250px-Map_of_present-day_Jharkhand_region_by_Rennell%2C_1779.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Map_of_present-day_Jharkhand_region_by_Rennell,_1779.jpg)Administrative jurisdictions of present-day Jharkhand region in 1770s
King [Ram Shah](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ram_Shah_\(king\) "Ram Shah \(king\)") ruled Navratangarh from 1640 to 1663. He built the [Kapilnath Temple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kapilnath_Temple "Kapilnath Temple") in 1643. He was succeeded by his son [Raghunath Shah](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raghunath_Shah "Raghunath Shah"). Thakur [Ani Nath Shahdeo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ani_Nath_Shahdeo "Ani Nath Shahdeo") built the Jagannath temple of Ranchi in 1691.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-44) The King [Medini Ray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medini_Ray "Medini Ray") ruled from 1658 to 1674 in Palamu.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahiry201424-45) His rule extended to areas in South [Gaya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaya_district "Gaya district") and [Hazaribagh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hazaribagh "Hazaribagh"). He attacked Navratangarh and defeated the [Nagvanshi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nagvanshi_Kingdom "Nagvanshi Kingdom") Maharaja of [Chhotanagpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Plateau "Chota Nagpur Plateau").[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-46) Chero rule in the [Palamu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palamu_division "Palamu division") region lasted until the 19th century until internal conflict between various factions weakened the Cheros and they were defeated by the [East India Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"). Later Palamu estates were sold by the British.[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-47)

During the 18th century, regions under the Chero dynasty, Nagvanshi dynasty, [Ramgarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramgarh_Raj "Ramgarh Raj") and [Kharagdiha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharagdiha "Kharagdiha") became territories of the [East India Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"). Ramgarh Raj along with estates of other chiefs in the regions were permanently settled as Zamindari estates. The [Kharagdiha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharagdiha "Kharagdiha") [Rajas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajas "Rajas") were settled as Rajas of Raj Dhanwar in 1809, and the Kharagdiha gadis were separately settled as zamindari estates. Some of the notable Kharagdiha Zamindari estates were [Koderma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koderma "Koderma"), [Gadi Palganj](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parasnath "Parasnath") and [Ledo Gadi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baghochia "Baghochia").[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-48) The princely states in the [Chota Nagpur Plateau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Plateau "Chota Nagpur Plateau") came within the [sphere of influence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sphere_of_influence "Sphere of influence") of the [Maratha Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy"), but they became tributary states of East India Company as a result of the [Anglo-Maratha Wars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anglo-Maratha_Wars_\(disambiguation\) "Anglo-Maratha Wars \(disambiguation\)") and became known as [Chota Nagpur Tributary States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Tributary_States "Chota Nagpur Tributary States").[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-49)
Subjugation, colonisation and imposition of taxes by the British East India Company resulted in spontaneous resistance from the local people. [Chuar Rebellion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chuar_Rebellion "Chuar Rebellion"), the first revolt against the British East India Company was led by Jagannath Singh Patar in 1767 with the [Bhumij](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhumij_people "Bhumij people") tribals. The Bhumijes again revolted in 1769–71, led by their Sardar Ghatwals in Dhalbhum. In 1769, [Raghunath Mahato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raghunath_Mahato "Raghunath Mahato") also revolted against the East India Company.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-50)
In 1771, a revolt against the landlords and the British government was led by [Tilka Majhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tilka_Manjhi "Tilka Manjhi"), a Paharia leader in Rajmahal Hills. Soon after in 1779, the [Bhumij](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhumij_people "Bhumij people") tribes again rose in arms against the British rule in Manbhum, called the Chuar Rebellion. In 1807, the Oraons in Barway murdered their landlord from [Srinagar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Srinagar "Srinagar"). The [Munda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munda_people "Munda people") tribe rose in revolt in 1811 and 1813. [Bakhtar Say](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bakhtar_Say "Bakhtar Say") and [Mundal Singh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mundal_Singh "Mundal Singh"), two landowners, fought against the British East India Company in 1812.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-51)
The Hos in Singhbhum revolted in 1820 and a [Kol revolt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kol_uprising "Kol uprising") occurred in 1832. Also in 1832 the [Bhumijs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhumij_people "Bhumij people") revolted again against the British, this time under the leadership of [Ganga Narayan Singh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganga_Narayan_Singh "Ganga Narayan Singh"), known as the [Bhumij rebellion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhumij_rebellion "Bhumij rebellion"). During the 19th century, large numbers of santals from [Manbhum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manbhum "Manbhum"), [Hazaribagh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hazaribagh "Hazaribagh"), [Midnapore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Midnapore "Midnapore") were settled by British in [Damin-i-koh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Damin-i-koh "Damin-i-koh") to cultivate the land and generate revenue. But the Santal revolted against the tax imposition. The [Santhal rebellion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santhal_rebellion "Santhal rebellion") broke out in 1855 under the leadership of two brothers [Sidhu and Kanhu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sidhu_and_Kanhu_Murmu "Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu"). Later the British renamed it Santal Pargana.[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-52)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Attack_by_600_Santhals_upon_a_party_of_50_sepoys%2C_40th_regiment_native_infantry.jpg/250px-Attack_by_600_Santhals_upon_a_party_of_50_sepoys%2C_40th_regiment_native_infantry.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Attack_by_600_Santhals_upon_a_party_of_50_sepoys,_40th_regiment_native_infantry.jpg)[Santhal rebellion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santhal_rebellion "Santhal rebellion") against tax imposition during British [Company Raj](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") in 1855
[Thakur Vishwanath Shahdeo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thakur_Vishwanath_Shahdeo "Thakur Vishwanath Shahdeo") and [Pandey Ganpat Rai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pandey_Ganpat_Rai "Pandey Ganpat Rai") rebelled against the British East India Company in the [1857 rebellion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"). In the [Battle of Chatra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Chatra "Battle of Chatra"), conflict took place between the rebels and the East India company.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-53)[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-54) [Tikait Umrao Singh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tikait_Umrao_Singh "Tikait Umrao Singh"), [Sheikh Bhikhari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sheikh_Bhikhari "Sheikh Bhikhari"), Nadir Ali and Jai Mangal Singh played pivotal role in the Indian Rebellion of 1857.[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-55) The brothers [Nilambar and Pitambar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nilambar_and_Pitambar "Nilambar and Pitambar") were chiefs of Bhogta clan of the [Kharwar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharwar "Kharwar") tribe who held ancestral jagirs with many [Chero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chero "Chero") Jagirdars and led revolt against the British East India company.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-latehar.nic.in-42)
After the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), the [rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") of the British [East India Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") was transferred to the Crown in the person of [Queen Victoria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"),[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-56) who, in 1876, was proclaimed [Empress of India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empress_of_India "Empress of India"). The [Cheros](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chero "Chero") and [Kharwars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharwar "Kharwar") again rebelled against the British in 1882 but the attack was repulsed.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-57) Then [Birsa Munda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birsa_Munda "Birsa Munda") revolt,[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-58) broke out in 1895 and lasted until 1900. The revolt though mainly concentrated in the [Munda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munda_people "Munda people") belt of Khunti, Tamar, Sarwada and Bandgaon. 
In October 1905, the exercise of British influence over the predominantly [Hindi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindi "Hindi")-speaking states of [Chang Bhakar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chang_Bhakar "Chang Bhakar"), [Jashpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jashpur_State "Jashpur State"), [Koriya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Korea_State "Korea State"), [Surguja](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surguja_State "Surguja State"), and [Udaipur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Udaipur_State,_Chhattisgarh "Udaipur State, Chhattisgarh") was transferred from the Bengal government to that of the [Central Provinces](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Provinces "Central Provinces"), while the two [Oriya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oriya_language "Oriya language")-speaking states of [Gangpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gangpur_State "Gangpur State") and [Bonai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bonai "Bonai") were attached to the [Orissa Tributary States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orissa_Tributary_States "Orissa Tributary States"), leaving only [Kharsawan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharsawan_State "Kharsawan State") and [Saraikela](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saraikela_State "Saraikela State") answerable to the Bengal governor.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-59)
In 1936, all nine states were transferred to the [Eastern States Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eastern_States_Agency "Eastern States Agency"), the officials of which came under the direct authority of the Governor-General of India, rather than under that of any provinces. 
In March 1940, the Indian National Congress (INC) 53rd Session[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-60)[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-61) occurred under the presidency of [Maulana Azad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maulana_Azad "Maulana Azad") at Jhanda Chowk, Ramgarh, which is now [Ramgarh Cantonment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramgarh_Cantonment "Ramgarh Cantonment"). [Mahatma Gandhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi"),[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-62) [Jawaharlal Nehru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru"), [Sardar Patel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sardar_Patel "Sardar Patel"), [Rajendra Prasad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajendra_Prasad "Rajendra Prasad"), [Sarojini Naidu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarojini_Naidu "Sarojini Naidu"), [Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan "Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan"), [Acharya J.B. Kripalani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acharya_J.B._Kripalani "Acharya J.B. Kripalani"), Industrialist [Jamnalal Bajaj](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamnalal_Bajaj "Jamnalal Bajaj") and other great leaders[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-63) of the Indian freedom movement attended the Ramgarh Session.[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-64) Mahatma Gandhi also opened the Khadi and Village Industries Exhibition at Ramgarh.[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-65)
At that time, under the leadership of Netaji [Subhash Chandra Bose](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subhas_Chandra_Bose "Subhas Chandra Bose") a conference against Samjhauta was also completed. In Ramgarh, Subhas Chandra Bose was seen as president of the All India Forward Block and M. N. Roy was seen as leader of the Radical democratic party. 
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Nehru_bajaj_sarojini_khan_azad1940a.jpg/250px-Nehru_bajaj_sarojini_khan_azad1940a.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Nehru_bajaj_sarojini_khan_azad1940a.jpg)Jawaharlal Nehru, industrialist Jamnalal Bajaj, Sarojini Naidu, Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan, and Maulana Azad at the 1940 Ramgarh session of the Indian National Congress.
### Post Independence
After Indian independence in 1947, the rulers of many states chose to accede to the [Dominion of India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India"). [Changbhakar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Changbhakar "Changbhakar"), [Jashpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jashpur "Jashpur"), [Koriya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koriya "Koriya"), [Surguja](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surguja "Surguja") and [Udaipur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Udaipur "Udaipur") after that became part of the state of [Madhya Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh"), but [Gangpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gangpur "Gangpur") and [Bonai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bonai "Bonai") became part of the state of [Orissa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orissa,_India "Orissa, India") and [Kharsawan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharsawan "Kharsawan") and [Saraikela](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saraikela "Saraikela") became part of the state of [Bihar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihar "Bihar").[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-66) In 1928, a separate state was demanded by _Unnati Samaj_ , the political wing of the Christian Tribals Association, which submitted a memorandum to the [Simon Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simon_Commission "Simon Commission") to constitute a tribal state in eastern India.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Gautam_Kumar_Bera-2008-25) Prominent leaders like [Jaipal Singh Munda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jaipal_Singh_Munda "Jaipal Singh Munda") and [Ram Narayan Singh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ram_Narayan_Singh "Ram Narayan Singh") also demanded a separate state. In 1955, the [Jharkhand Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand_Party "Jharkhand Party"), led by Jaipal Singh Munda, submitted a memorandum to the [States Reorganization Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/States_Reorganization_Commission "States Reorganization Commission") for a separate Jharkhand state comprising the tribal area of South Bihar, but it was rejected because there were many languages, no link language in the region, tribals were in the minority, Hindustani was the majority language, and it risked adverse effect on the economy of Bihar.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Concept_Publishing_Company-67)[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-68)
Later the [Sadan people](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sadan_people "Sadan people"), the native various caste/non-tribal groups, also joined the movement for a separate state.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Concept_Publishing_Company-67) In 1972, [Binod Bihari Mahato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binod_Bihari_Mahato "Binod Bihari Mahato"), [Shibu Soren](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shibu_Soren "Shibu Soren") and [A. K. Roy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A._K._Roy "A. K. Roy") founded [Jharkhand Mukti Morcha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand_Mukti_Morcha "Jharkhand Mukti Morcha"). [Nirmal Mahto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nirmal_Mahto "Nirmal Mahto") founded the [All Jharkhand Students Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/All_Jharkhand_Students_Union "All Jharkhand Students Union"). They led the movement for a separate state of Jharkhand. The Jharkhand coordination committee (JCC), consisting of [Ram Dayal Munda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ram_Dayal_Munda "Ram Dayal Munda"), [B. P. Keshri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bisheshwar_Prasad_Keshari "Bisheshwar Prasad Keshari"), [Binod Bihari Mahato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binod_Bihari_Mahato "Binod Bihari Mahato"), Santosh Rana and Suraj Singh Besra started a new initiative and tried to coordinate between different parties. Keshri sent a memorandum to form Jharkhand state in 1988.[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-69) The Jharkhand co-ordination committee was then led by Congress General Secretary [Ram Ratan Ram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ram_Ratan_Ram "Ram Ratan Ram"), who urged [Rajiv Gandhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajiv_Gandhi "Rajiv Gandhi") to pay attention to the issue at hand. 
In July 1988, the [Bharatiya Janata party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_party "Bharatiya Janata party") led by [Atal Bihari Vajpayee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atal_Bihari_Vajpayee "Atal Bihari Vajpayee"), [Lal Krishna Advani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lal_Krishna_Advani "Lal Krishna Advani"), and [Murli Manohar Joshi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murli_Manohar_Joshi "Murli Manohar Joshi") decided to demand a separate state, Vanachal, comprising the forest region of South Bihar in [Jamshedpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamshedpur "Jamshedpur"). [Inder Singh Namdhari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inder_Singh_Namdhari "Inder Singh Namdhari"), [Samresh Singh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samresh_Singh "Samresh Singh"), and [Rudra Pratap Sarangi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rudra_Pratap_Sarangi "Rudra Pratap Sarangi") were the leaders of the Vanachal movement. They organised several rallies to form a separate state.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-jagran2018-70)
The central government formed a committee on the Jharkhand matter in 1989. It stressed the need for greater allocation of development funds for the area. There was a provision for limited internal autonomy in the hill area of [Assam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assam "Assam"). Other [tribal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tribal "Tribal") areas were covered by the fifth schedule of the constitution. Chotanagpur and [Santal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santal "Santal") Pargana development boards were constituted under the chairmanship of the then chief minister of Bihar under the provision of the fifth schedule in 1972. This failed to achieve the desired result. Jharkhand Mukti Morcha wanted more representation and the All Jharkhand Students Union was against it. Due to differences these parties broke away from each other. The All Jharkhand Students Union introduced elements of violence in the movement and called for a boycott of election while Jharkhand Mukti Morcha opposed this. The Jharkhand Area Autonomous Council bill passed in Bihar's legislative assembly in December 1994. The Jharkhand Area Autonomous Council were given responsibility for forty areas including agriculture, rural health, public work, public health and minerals. The council has power to recommend legislation to the Assembly through the state government and to frame bylaws and regulations.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-https-39)[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-Concept_Publishing_Company-67)
In 1998, when the separate state movement was falling apart, Justice [Lal Pingley Nath Shahdeo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lal_Pingley_Nath_Shahdeo "Lal Pingley Nath Shahdeo") was leading the movement. In 1998, the [Union government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Union_government "Union government") decided to send the bill concerning the formation of the state of Jharkhand to the [Bihar Legislative Assembly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihar_Legislative_Assembly "Bihar Legislative Assembly") to which [Lalu Prasad Yadav](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lalu_Prasad_Yadav "Lalu Prasad Yadav") had said that the state would be divided over his dead body. A total of 16 political parties including the [Bharatiya Janata Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party"), Jharkhand Mukti Morcha, the [All Jharkhand Students Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/All_Jharkhand_Students_Union "All Jharkhand Students Union"), and the [Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") came in one platform and formed the 'All Party Separate State Formation Committee' to start the movement. Shahdeo was elected as the convener of the committee. Voting on the Jharkhand Act was to be done on 21 September 1998 in the Bihar legislature. On that day the committee, under the leadership of Shahdeo called for Jharkhand Bandh and organised a protest march. Thousands of supporters of a separate state took to the streets led by Shahdeo. He was arrested and detained in a police station for hours along with many supporters.[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-71)[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-72)
In 1999 the Bharatiya Janata party promised to form a separate Vanachal state if they won the state election with a majority of votes.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-jagran2018-70) After the last Assembly election in the state resulted in a hung assembly, [RJD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rashtriya_Janata_Dal "Rashtriya Janata Dal")'s dependence on the Congress extended support on the precondition that RJD would not pose a hurdle to the passage of the [Bihar reorganisation Bill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihar_Reorganisation_Act,_2000 "Bihar Reorganisation Act, 2000"). Finally, with the support from both RJD and [Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"), the ruling coalition at the Centre led by the Bharatiya Janata Party which had made statehood its main poll plank in the region in successive polls earlier, cleared the Bihar reorganisation Bill in the monsoon session of the Parliament on 2 and 11 August in Loksabha and Rajyasabha. This paved the way for the creation of a separate Vanachal state comprising the [Chota Nagpur Division](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Division "Chota Nagpur Division") and [Santhal Pargana Division](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santhal_Pargana_Division "Santhal Pargana Division") of South Bihar.[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-73) [NDA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Democratic_Alliance_\(India\) "National Democratic Alliance \(India\)") formed the government with [Babulal Marandi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Babulal_Marandi "Babulal Marandi") as chief minister. Later the name of the state was changed from Vanachal to Jharkhand.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-jagran2018-70)[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-74) [Babulal Marandi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Babulal_Marandi "Babulal Marandi") took the oath of chief minister on 15 November 2000 on the anniversary of the birth of tribal leader [Birsa Munda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birsa_Munda "Birsa Munda").[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-https-39)
### Jharkhand statehood
The dynamics of resources and the politics of development still influence the socio-economic structures in Jharkhand, which was carved out of the relatively underdeveloped southern part of [Bihar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"). According to the 1991 census, the state has a population of over 20 million out of which 28% is [tribal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tribal "Tribal") while 12% of the people belong to [scheduled castes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scheduled_castes_and_scheduled_tribes "Scheduled castes and scheduled tribes"). Jharkhand has 24 districts, 260 blocks, and 32,620 villages out of which only 45% have access to electricity while only 8,484 are connected by roads. Jharkhand is the leading producer of mineral wealth in the country after [Chhattisgarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhattisgarh "Chhattisgarh") state, endowed as it is with a vast variety of minerals like iron ore, coal, copper ore, mica, bauxite, graphite, limestone, and uranium. Jharkhand is also known for its vast forest resources.[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-75)
### Naxal insurgency
See also: [Tritiya Prastuti Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tritiya_Prastuti_Committee "Tritiya Prastuti Committee") and [People's Liberation Front of India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Front_of_India "People's Liberation Front of India")
Jharkhand has been at the centre of the [Naxalite–Maoist insurgency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naxalite%E2%80%93Maoist_insurgency "Naxalite–Maoist insurgency"). Since the uprising of the [Naxalites](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naxalites "Naxalites") in 1967, 6,000 people have been killed in fighting between the Naxalites and counter-insurgency operations by the [police](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand_Police "Jharkhand Police"), and [paramilitary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") groups such as the [Salwa Judum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salwa_Judum "Salwa Judum").[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-76)
Jharkhand is part of the "[Red Corridor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Corridor "Red Corridor")", which comprises 92,000 square kilometres, where the highest concentration of the group's estimated 20,000 combatants fight.[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-globalpolitician.com-77)[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-78) Part of this is due to the fact that the state harbours an abundance of natural resources, while its people live in abject poverty and destitution.[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-atimes.com-79) The impoverished state provides ample recruits for [communist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communist "Communist") insurgents, who argue that they are fighting on behalf of the landless poor and tribals that see few benefits from the resource extractions.[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-atimes.com-79) As the federal government holds a monopoly on sub-surface resources in the state, the [tribal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tribal "Tribal") population is prevented from staking any claim on the resources extracted from their land.[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-atimes.com-79) In response, the insurgents have recently[_[when?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")_] begun a campaign of targeting infrastructure related to the extraction of resources vital for Indian energy needs, such as coal.[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-globalpolitician.com-77)
On 5 March 2007, [Sunil Mahato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunil_Kumar_Mahato "Sunil Kumar Mahato"), a member of the national parliament, was killed by [Naxalite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naxalite "Naxalite") rebels near [Kishanpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kishanpur "Kishanpur") while watching a football match on the [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") festival of [Holi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holi "Holi"). His widow, [Suman Mahato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suman_Mahato "Suman Mahato"), the [Jharkhand Mukti Morcha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand_Mukti_Morcha "Jharkhand Mukti Morcha") candidate, won the [Jamshedpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamshedpur "Jamshedpur") Lok Sabha by-election in September 2007 and served in parliament until 2009.[[80]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-80)
## Geography
Jharkhand is located in the eastern part of India and is enclosed by [West Bengal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") to the east, [Chhattisgarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhattisgarh "Chhattisgarh") and [Uttar Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") to the west, [Bihar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") to the north and [Odisha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Odisha "Odisha") to the south. 
Jharkhand envelops a geographical area of 79,716 square kilometres (30,779 sq mi). Much of Jharkhand lies on the [Chota Nagpur Plateau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Plateau "Chota Nagpur Plateau"). Many rivers pass through the Chota Nagpur plateau. They are: [Damodar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Damodar_River "Damodar River"), [North Koel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Koel_River "North Koel River"), [Barakar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barakar "Barakar"), [South Koel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Koel_River "South Koel River"), [Sankh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sankh_River "Sankh River") and [Subarnarekha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subarnarekha_River "Subarnarekha River") rivers. The higher watersheds of these rivers stretch out within the Jharkhand state. Much of the Jharkhand state is still enclosed by forest. Forests sustain the population of elephants and tigers. 
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/India_Jharkhand_relief_map.png/250px-India_Jharkhand_relief_map.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:India_Jharkhand_relief_map.png)Physical map of Jharkhand
### Climate
Climate of Jharkhand varies from [humid subtropical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") in the north to [tropical wet and dry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate") in the south-east.[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-81) The main seasons are summer, rainy, autumn, winter and spring. The summer lasts from mid-April to mid-June. May, the hottest month, characterised by daily high temperatures around 37 °C (99 °F) and low temperatures around 25 °C (77 °F). The [southwest monsoon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monsoon_of_South_Asia "Monsoon of South Asia"), from mid-June to October, brings nearly all the state's annual rainfall, which ranges from about 1,000 mm (40 in) in the west-central part of the state to more than 1,500 mm (60 in) in the southwest. Nearly half of the annual precipitation falls in July and August. The winter season lasts from November to February. The temperatures in [Ranchi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi "Ranchi") in December usually vary from 10–24 °C (50–75 °F). Spring season lasts from mid-February to mid-April.[[82]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-82)
### Hills and mountain ranges
  * [Parasnath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parasnath "Parasnath"): Parasnath Hill is also called as [Sri Sammed Sikharji](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sri_Sammed_Sikharji "Sri Sammed Sikharji") by Jains and [Marang Buru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marang_Buru "Marang Buru") by [Santal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santal "Santal") tribes. The Parasnath Hill is situated in Giridih district of Jharkhand. It is a chief [Jain pilgrimage site](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jain_pilgrimage_site "Jain pilgrimage site") and the holy place for [Jains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jain "Jain"). Additionally, for [Santal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santal "Santal") tribes, their supreme deity [Marang Buru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marang_Buru "Marang Buru") is prayed and _Jug Jaher_ is the holy enshrine in its valley where _Jaher Ayo_ being worshiped. It is believed in the Jain culture that 20 of the 24 Tirthankaras attained Moksha from this place. The height of the hill is 1,365 meters.
  * [Netarhat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netarhat "Netarhat"): Netarhat is a town in Latehar district. Referred to as the "Queen of Chotanagpur", it is a hill station. [Netarhat Residential School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netarhat_Residential_School "Netarhat Residential School") is located here. Netarhat Dam is also located in this area.
  * [Rajmahal Hills](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajmahal_Hills "Rajmahal Hills"): These hills are located in [Sahibganj](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sahibganj "Sahibganj") and [Godda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Godda "Godda") districts of Eastern part of Jharkhand. The [Rajmahal hills](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajmahal_hills "Rajmahal hills") belong to the Jurassic era. These hills like others also have many waterfalls, lakes and greenery.
  * [Trikut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trikut "Trikut"): Trikut Hill is located ten kilometres away from Deoghar and lies on the way to Dumka in Jharkhand. Trikut hill is also called Trikutchal because there are 3 major peaks on the hill. The height of Trikut hill is 2470 feet.
  * [Tagore Hill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tagore_Hill "Tagore Hill"): The [Tagore Hill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tagore_Hill "Tagore Hill") is also recognised as the Morabadi Hill. The Tagore hill is located in Morabadi, Ranchi. The brother of Rabindranath Tagore, Jyotirindranath Tagore had made a tour at Ranchi in the year 1908.[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-83)


### Main Rivers
Further information: [List of rivers of Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Jharkhand "List of rivers of Jharkhand")
  * [Ganga River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganga_River "Ganga River"): The [holy river](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holy_river "Holy river") Ganga passes through the north-eastern district of Sahebganj. Cities on the banks of Ganga river in Jharkhand: Sahebganj, Rajmahal
  * [Son River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Son_River "Son River"): Origin of Son River: Amarkantak, Cities on the Shore of Son River: Sidhi
  * [Subarnarekha River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subarnarekha_River "Subarnarekha River"): Origin of Subarnarekha River: (Nagdi Ranchi) Chota Nagpur Plateau, Cities on the Shore of Subarnarekha River: Ranchi, Chandil, Jamshedpur, Ghatshila, Gopiballavpur
  * [Kharkai River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kharkai_River "Kharkai River"): Origin of Kharkai River: Mayurbhanj District, Odisha; Cities on the Shore of Kharkai River: Rairangpur, Adityapur, and enters the Subarnarekha river in north-western Jamshedpur.
  * [Damodar River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Damodar_River "Damodar River"): Origin of Damodar River: Chota Nagpur Plateau (Tori latehar), Cities on the Shore of Damodar River: latehar, lohardaga, Ramgarh, Gridih, Dhanbad, Bokaro, Asansol, Raniganj, Durgapur, Bardhaman
  * [North Koel River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Koel_River "North Koel River"): Origin of North Koel River: Chota Nagpur plateau, Cities on Shore of North Koel River: Daltonganj
  * [South Koel River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Koel_River "South Koel River"): Origin of South Koyal River: Chota Nagpur Plateau (Nagdi Ranchi), Cities on the Shore of South Koyal River: Manoharpur, Rourkela
  * [Lilajan River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lilajan_River "Lilajan River"): Also known as Falgu river. Origin of Lilajan River: Northern Chota Nagpur Plateau, City on the Shore: Gaya
  * [Ajay River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajay_River "Ajay River"): Origin of Ajay River: Munger, Cities on the Shore of Ajay River: Purulia, Chittaranjan, Ilambazar, Jaydev Kenduli
  * [Mayurakshi River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mayurakshi_River "Mayurakshi River"): Origin of Mayurakshi River: Trikut hill, City on the Shore of Mayurakshi River: Suri
  * [Barakar River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barakar_River "Barakar River"): Origin: Padma in Hazaribagh, Barakar Nadi flows through the districts of Koderma, Giridih, Hazaribagh, etc.


For the list of dams built across these revere refer to [[2]](https://indiawris.gov.in/wiki/doku.php?id=dams_in_jharkhand)
### Flora and Fauna
Jharkhand has a rich variety of [flora](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flora_\(plants\) "Flora \(plants\)") and [fauna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fauna_\(animals\) "Fauna \(animals\)"). The wildlife sanctuaries, national parks and zoological gardens in the state present a panorama of this variety. There are several wildlife sanctuaries and national park in the state, including [Hazaribag Wildlife Sanctuary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hazaribag_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Hazaribag Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Dalma Wildlife Sanctuary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dalma_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Dalma Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Koderma Wildlife Sanctuary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koderma_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Koderma Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Lawalong Wildlife Sanctuary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lawalong_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Lawalong Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Palkot Wildlife Sanctuary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palkot_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Palkot Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahuadanr_Wolf_Sanctuary "Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary"), [Palamu Tiger Reserve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palamu_Tiger_Reserve "Palamu Tiger Reserve") and [Betla National Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betla_National_Park "Betla National Park").[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-84)[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-85) Palamu Tiger Reserve reserve is abode to hundreds of species of flora and fauna,[[86]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-86) as indicated within brackets: mammals (39), snakes (8), lizards (4), fish (6), insects (21), birds (170), seed bearing plants and trees (97), shrubs and herbs (46), climbers, parasites and semi-parasites (25), and grasses and bamboos (17).

According to the [2011 Indian Census](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_census_of_India "2011 census of India"), Jharkhand has a population of 32.96 million, consisting of 16.93 million males and 16.03 million females.[[88]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-2011census-88) The [sex ratio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_sex_ratio "Human sex ratio") is 947 females to 1,000 males.[[88]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-2011census-88) In 2017 The literacy rate of the state was 73.40% with [Ranchi district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi_district "Ranchi district") being most educated at 83.13% compared to rural Pakur district being least at 50.17%.[[88]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand#cite_note-2011census-88) In social demographics, Jharkhand's [Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scheduled_Castes_and_Scheduled_Tribes "Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes") populations are 3,985,644 (12.08%) and 8,646,189 (26.21%), respectively. Nationally, they rank 14th and 6th, comprising 1.98% and 8.29% of the total population within these social groups. They are predominantly concentrated in south-western district [Simdega](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simdega_district "Simdega district") (78.23%), [Khunti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khunti_district "Khunti district") (77.77%), [Gumla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gumla_district "Gumla district") (72.11%), [Paschim Singhbhum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Singhbhum_district "West Singhbhum district") (71.1%), [Latehar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latehar_district "Latehar district") (66.85%), and in [Lohardaga district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lohardaga_district "Lohardaga district") (60.21%).
